text: This is a robotic ETEP ventral hernia repair complicated by posterior fascial dehiscence and a strategy for management. We have nothing to disclose. This is a fifty-one year old male presenting with a painful recurrent ventral hernia. Past medical history includes chronic constipation and obstructive sleep apnea. Past surgical history includes a laparoscopic eye palm complicated by mesh infection, followed by laparoscopic removal of mesh, followed by hernia recurrence. Preoperative CT scan obtained reveals a significant diastasis ranging from five to six centimeters. The rectus muscles were robust, measuring about six centimeters on either side. There appeared to be a few small midline fascial defects, with the largest measuring three centimeters.

The surgical team uses a DaVinci XI robot for a robotic retromuscular abdominal wall hernia repair. The retro-rectus space is accessed using a 10mm OptiView trocar, followed by blunt dissection with the trocar and insufflation. Two additional 8mm ports are placed, and the robot is docked. The right rectus space is large, allowing the team to work medially across to the linea alba along the length of the rectus muscle.

The surgeon incises the posterior rectus sheath superior to the hernia to access the preperitoneal space above the falciform ligament, crossing to the opposite side. The incision is made on the patient's left side, and similarly, the posterior rectus sheath is incised across the arcuate line into the preperitoneal space below the hernia, isolating the hernia sac, which is then opened.

The surgeon circumferentially dissects the hernia sac, revealing it to be larger than previously noted, approximately six centimeters. The dissection extends into the left retroactive space, developing it further. The posterior rectus sheath is reassessed, showing sufficient laxity for tension-free closure.

The surgeon closes the diastasis and ventral hernia from superior to inferior using a running number one Stratifix suture, carefully imbricating the hernia sac without including skin. The tension on the stitches is managed by sequentially tightening them after reducing abdominal pressure. The process is repeated from inferior to superior, meeting in the middle. The posterior rectus fascia is then closed with a two L V-lock suture in a running fashion from bottom to top, ensuring no undue tension.

The surgeon measures the retrorectus space, cutting a monofilament polypropylene macroporous mesh into an ovoid shape and placing it within the space. The mesh fits well without requiring fixation. Hemostasis is surveyed, and a 19 French Blake drain is left in place after desufflation.

The surgeon performs a diagnostic laparoscopy using three trocar sites from the initial operation. The posterior fascial closure is observed to be widely disrupted, with a loop of small bowel adherent to the mesh. The small bowel is carefully reduced and appears viable.
The surgeon observes a large fascial defect, likely exacerbated by the patient's abdominal distension. A composite macroporous mesh is placed in an intraperitoneal onlay fashion to close the defect.
After this operation, the patient did well and was discharged home post-op day three after return of bowel function.
